If you notice persistent cognitive issues despite CPAP use, discuss with your doctor, you may need treatment adjustments.

weight_loss.title: Follow an anti-inflammatory diet
weight_loss.description:
Chronic inflammation can worsen sleep apnea by increasing airway swelling. An anti-inflammatory diet rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and healthy fats can help reduce this inflammation.

Focus on:

  • Omega-3 rich foods (salmon, walnuts, flaxseeds)
  • Antioxidant-rich fruits and vegetables
  • Whole grains instead of refined carbs
  • Lean proteins

This approach supports weight loss while directly addressing inflammation that may contribute to sleep apnea.

weight_loss.title: Avoid large meals 2-3 hours before bed
weight_loss.description:
Large meals before bedtime can worsen sleep apnea by pushing up on the diaphragm and reducing lung capacity. They also increase the risk of acid reflux, which can further irritate the airway.

If you're hungry before bed, choose a light, protein-rich snack like Greek yogurt or a small handful of nuts. Avoid heavy, fatty, or spicy foods that can cause digestive issues and disrupt sleep.

weight_loss.title: Reduce processed foods and added sugars
weight_loss.description:
Processed foods and added sugars contribute to weight gain and inflammation, both of which worsen sleep apnea. They also cause blood sugar spikes and crashes that can disrupt sleep patterns.

Focus on whole, minimally processed foods. Read labels and aim to reduce added sugars to less than 25g per day. This supports sustainable weight loss and better overall health.

weight_loss.title: Stay hydrated, but limit evening fluids
weight_loss.description:
Proper hydration is important for overall health and can help with CPAP humidification. However, limit fluids 2 hours before bedtime to reduce nighttime bathroom trips that disrupt sleep.

If you use a CPAP humidifier, proper daytime hydration helps prevent dry mouth and nasal passages. Sip water throughout the day rather than drinking large amounts in the evening.

sleep_apnea_foods.title: Avoid foods that cause acid reflux
sleep_apnea_foods.description:
Acid reflux (GERD) is common in sleep apnea patients and can worsen symptoms by irritating the airway. Avoid trigger foods like:

  • Spicy foods
  • Citrus fruits and juices
  • Tomatoes and tomato-based products
  • Chocolate
  • High-fat foods
  • Caffeine

If you experience reflux, elevate your head while sleeping and consider discussing medication with your doctor.

sleep_apnea_foods.title: Eat magnesium-rich foods
sleep_apnea_foods.description:
Magnesium plays a role in muscle relaxation and may help with sleep quality. Some research suggests magnesium deficiency may be linked to sleep apnea severity.

Include magnesium-rich foods like:

  • Leafy greens (spinach, kale)
  • Nuts and seeds
  • Legumes
  • Whole grains
  • Dark chocolate (in moderation)

Talk to your doctor before taking magnesium supplements, as they can interact with medications.

sleep_apnea_foods.title: Consider a Mediterranean-style diet
sleep_apnea_foods.description:
The Mediterranean diet has been associated with reduced sleep apnea severity. It emphasizes:

  • Fruits and vegetables
  • Whole grains
  • Healthy fats (olive oil, nuts)
  • Lean proteins (fish, poultry)
  • Limited red meat and processed foods

This eating pattern supports weight loss, reduces inflammation, and provides nutrients that support overall sleep health.

cpap.title: Choose the right CPAP mask type
cpap.description:
There are three main CPAP mask types:

  • Nasal masks: Cover only the nose, good for mouth breathers who can train themselves
  • Full face masks: Cover nose and mouth, ideal for mouth breathers
  • Nasal pillows: Small cushions that sit at the nostrils, least intrusive

Work with your DME provider to find the best fit. Many patients try multiple types before finding their ideal mask. Comfort is key to compliance.

cpap.title: Use CPAP humidification
cpap.description:
CPAP humidifiers add moisture to the pressurized air, preventing dry mouth, nasal congestion, and throat irritation. This is especially important for patients who experience these side effects.

Most modern CPAP machines have integrated humidifiers with adjustable heat settings. Start with a moderate setting and adjust based on comfort. Heated tubing can prevent condensation (rainout) in your mask.

cpap.title: Replace CPAP supplies regularly
cpap.description:
Regular replacement ensures optimal performance and hygiene:

  • Mask cushions: Every 1-2 weeks
  • Filters: Monthly
  • Tubing: Every 3 months
  • Headgear: Every 6 months
  • Water chamber: Every 6 months

Most insurance plans cover these replacements. Worn equipment can cause leaks, reduce effectiveness, and increase infection risk.

cpap.title: Consider APAP or BiPAP if CPAP is uncomfortable
cpap.description:
If you struggle with CPAP pressure, discuss alternatives with your sleep doctor:

  • APAP (Auto-CPAP): Automatically adjusts pressure throughout the night based on your needs
  • BiPAP: Provides different pressures for inhalation and exhalation, often more comfortable

These machines may improve comfort and compliance, especially for patients who find constant CPAP pressure difficult to tolerate.

supportive.title: Use a CPAP-friendly pillow
supportive.description:
Special CPAP pillows have cutouts or contours that accommodate your mask and prevent dislodgement during sleep. They're particularly helpful for side sleepers who may push their mask off against a regular pillow.

Look for pillows made of memory foam or other materials that maintain their shape and provide proper head and neck support while working with your CPAP setup.

supportive.title: Consider a CPAP machine with data tracking
supportive.description:
Modern CPAP machines with built-in data tracking can monitor your usage, leak rates, and AHI. This information helps you and your doctor optimize your treatment.

Many machines sync with smartphone apps, making it easy to track your progress and identify issues. This data is invaluable for troubleshooting compliance problems and adjusting therapy.

supportive.title: Use a white noise machine
supportive.description:
A white noise machine can mask CPAP machine sounds and environmental noise, promoting deeper sleep. While modern CPAPs are quiet, some users are sensitive to any noise.

White noise also helps maintain sleep continuity, which is important for sleep apnea patients who already experience fragmented sleep. Choose a machine with adjustable volume and tone.

supportive.title: Keep a sleep tracking device
supportive.description:
While not a replacement for medical sleep studies, sleep trackers can help you monitor sleep patterns, duration, and quality. Some devices can detect snoring and may provide insights into your sleep apnea management.

Use tracking data to identify patterns and discuss trends with your sleep doctor. However, remember that these devices aren't diagnostic tools and shouldn't replace professional medical care.

digital.title: Establish screen timing rules before bed
digital.description:
Implement a "no screens" rule for at least 1 hour before bedtime. If you must use devices, enable night mode (red/warm filters) and keep brightness minimal. The goal is to avoid blue light exposure that suppresses melatonin production.

For sleep apnea patients, the temptation to use screens while adjusting to CPAP can be strong, resist this. Screen use before bed delays sleep onset, reducing your total sleep time and the benefits of CPAP therapy. Consider replacing screen time with reading (physical books or e-readers with warm light), gentle stretching, or listening to calming audio.

digital.title: Control content intensity in the evening
digital.description:
Avoid stimulating or emotionally intense content (thriller shows, work emails, news, social media arguments) in the 2-3 hours before bed. This type of content increases arousal, stress, and cognitive activation, making sleep onset difficult.

Instead, consume calming content: nature documentaries, light reading, soothing music, or guided meditations. The goal is to transition your brain from an active, alert state to a relaxed, sleep-ready state. For sleep apnea patients, this is especially important because stress and arousal can worsen sleep quality even with effective CPAP treatment.

digital.title: Manage dopamine load from digital stimulation
digital.description:
Digital devices are designed to deliver intermittent rewards that trigger dopamine release, keeping you engaged and alert. Reduce dopamine-stimulating activities (social media scrolling, gaming, online shopping) in the evening to allow your brain to wind down.

Consider using app blockers or screen time limits to enforce boundaries. The constant novelty and reward anticipation from digital content keeps your brain in a state of alertness incompatible with sleep. For better sleep hygiene, replace evening digital stimulation with low-arousal activities like reading, journaling, or gentle conversation.

digital.title: Create notification suppression windows
digital.description:
Set your devices to "Do Not Disturb" mode 1-2 hours before bed and keep it on until morning. This prevents notifications from disrupting your wind-down routine or waking you during sleep. Critical calls can be set to bypass this, but most notifications can wait.

Sleep apnea patients already experience frequent sleep disruptions, adding notification alerts worsens sleep fragmentation. Place phones in another room or use airplane mode to create physical and psychological separation from digital interruptions during your sleep period.
